summary: Gruber responds to criticism that he only cares about benchmarks when they favor Apple, arguing that iPhone 7's A-series chip performance is genuinely significant. He acknowledges he'd use an iPhone even if it were slower, just as he used Macs during their slower PowerPC years. The key insight is that Apple's in-house chip design creates an exclusive advantage: if commodity chips were faster, Apple would just use those, but since Apple's own chips are fastest, only Apple benefits. This sustained, growing performance lead combined with the iPhone's existing design advantages makes the situation historically extraordinary.
key_points: Gruber used Macs throughout the PowerPC era despite inferior performance, proving his platform loyalty isn't benchmark-driven
If Apple's chips were slower than commodity alternatives, Apple would simply use commodity chips like they do on desktop — the fact that they don't proves the A-series advantage is real
The A-series chips create an asymmetric advantage: commodity chips are available to everyone, but Apple's superior chips are exclusive to Apple
The performance lead is growing year-over-year, making it a sustained structural advantage rather than a fluke
money_quotes: If Apple's in-house chips were significantly slower than the commodity chips used by Android device makers, Apple would just use those commodity chips.
If the commodity chips were fastest, everyone would use them. But if the A-series chips are the fastest (and have the best energy efficiency), only Apple gets to use them.
The iPhone has all the benefits (in short: superior design) that would keep me, and I think most other iPhone users, on the platform even if it didn't have a performance advantage.
This is an extraordinary situation, historically. And year-over-year, it looks like Apple's lead is growing, not shrinking.
It's not a fluke, but a sustained advantage.
I never argued then that performance didn't matter — only that for me, personally, the other benefits of using a Mac outweighed the performance penalty Macs suffered.
key_insight: Apple's A-series chip dominance creates an unprecedented exclusive advantage — if commodity chips were better Apple would use them, but since Apple's own chips lead, only Apple benefits from the best mobile silicon.
